text: The surgeon introduces the reusable transanal DAPRI port into the anus to treat a benign rectal stricture. A silicon cap is applied, facilitating the use of a 10mm, 30-degree, regular length scope alongside a reusable curved DAPRI instrument.

The surgeon visualizes the benign rectal stenosis and introduces a monocurve reusable grasping forceps through the port's orifice at the nine o'clock position. A measuring straight grasper is inserted at the three o'clock position, confirming the lesion is 2.5 centimeters from the anal margin.

A monocurve reusable coagulating hook is inserted at the three o'clock position. The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a circumferential tattoo around the stenosis. Dissection starts with a mucosal opening at the six o'clock position, extending laterally on both sides and then moving anteriorly.
The surgeon uses a coagulating hook to gently push the muscle layer laterally, revealing the submucosal plane posteriorly.

The surgeon continues the submucosal dissection cranially, ensuring the mucosal layer remains preserved at the bottom. A coagulating hook is used to facilitate this dissection, demonstrating its utility in the procedure.

The surgeon continues the dissection under ergonomic conditions, preserving the muscle layer laterally while joining the previous dissection anteriorly and posteriorly. The muscular fibers are well exposed as the dissection progresses posteriorly, laterally, and anteriorly.

The final view of the dissection reveals the introduction of the coagulating hook into the mucosal lumen, traversing over the stenosis. The distal margin of the dissected mucosa is cut, and the specimen is removed through the D-port. The full length of the stenosis is visibly displayed.
The surgeon controls the entire muscular layer and the distal mucosal margin, testing the feasibility of suture repair.

The surgeon introduces a monocurve reusable needle holder through the port orifice at a three o'clock position and inserts the needle through the central port orifice. The repair begins with the first suture placed at a six o'clock position, passing the suture through the inferior and superior edges. Intercorporeal sutures and knots are performed under ergonomic conditions, allowing freedom of movement within the lumen.

The surgeon inserts monocurve reusable scissors through the port's orifice at the three o'clock position to cut the first suture. A new suture is placed at the twelve o'clock position, with the needle passing through the inferior edge and then into the superior edge, followed by the formation of an intercorporeal knot.

The surgeon places another suture at the two o'clock position, demonstrating smooth hand coordination without conflict. The final result of the closure is visible, indicating the completion of the procedure.
